article.title: High Temperature Alters Leaf Lipid Membrane Composition Associated with Photochemistry of PSII and Membrane Thermostability in Rice Seedlings
article.license: Licensee MDPI, Basel, Switzerland. This article is an open access article distributed under the terms and conditions of the Creative Commons Attribution (CC BY) license (https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/).

events.event_label: control condition improves membrane stability (low EL)
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p012.s001: All four rice genotypes under the control condition displayed good membrane stability as indicated by low EL values ranging from 3.24% to 4.16% (Figure 2A). PMC9183074.p012.s002: When subjected to HT stress, cellular membranes were damaged, and all rice genotypes showed significantly higher EL. PMC9183074.p012.s003: The membranes of the heat-sensitive check, IR64, was most seriously damaged, showing a nine-fold increase in EL, while those of N22 showed the highest integrity with only a 7.15% increase in EL. PMC9183074.p012.s004: Of the two Thai cultivars, CN1 maintained greater membrane stability with its EL value similar to that of N22.

events.event_label: HT increases abundance of arachidic, behenic and lignoceric acids
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p017.s006: Palmitic acid and stearic acid markedly decreased with HT in all cultivars. PMC9183074.p017.s007: Three saturated fatty acids including arachidic acid, behenic acid, and lignoceric acid in N22, CN1 and IR64 were significantly increased after HT treatment, but in KDML105, the contents of these acids declined significantly.

events.event_label: HT induces ROS causing membrane injury and impaired photosynthesis
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p024.s001: High temperature (HT) is harmful to plant growth and survival. PMC9183074.p024.s002: It decreases photosynthetic efficiency and yield. PMC9183074.p024.s003: HT can induce excessive reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation, resulting in cellular membrane injury, impaired photosynthetic mechanism and oxygen evolving complex associated with PSII, and inhibition of protein synthesis. PMC9183074.p024.s004: Plants can adapt to HT by changing the morphology and altering metabolism via signal transduction.

events.event_label: High temperature alters leaf lipid membrane and reduces membrane thermostability
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p001.s001: High Temperature Alters Leaf Lipid Membrane Composition Associated with Photochemistry of PSII and Membrane Thermostability in Rice Seedlings

events.event_label: High temperature reduces electrolyte leakage and malondialdehyde accumulation
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p002.s003: Twenty-eight-day-old seedlings of two Thai rice cultivars (CN1 and KDML105), a standard heat tolerance (N22), and a heat sensitive (IR64) rice genotype were treated at 42 °C for 7 days. PMC9183074.p002.s004: Under HT, N22 showed the highest heat tolerance displaying the lowest increase in electrolyte leakage (EL), no increments in malondialdehyde (MDA) and stable maximum quantum yield of PSII efficiency (Fv/Fm). PMC9183074.p002.s005: Compared to KDML105 and IR64, CN1 was more tolerant of HT, showing a lower increase in EL and MDA, and less reduction in Fv/Fm. PMC9183074.p002.s006: N22 and CN1 showed a higher percentage reduction of unsaturated fatty acids (C18:2 and C18:3), which are the major components of the thylakoid membrane, rendering the optimum thylakoid membrane fluidity and intactness of PSII complex. PMC9183074.p002.s007: Moreover, they exhibited sharp increases in long-chain fatty acids, particularly C22:1, while the heat sensitive IR64 and KDML105 showed significant reductions.

events.event_label: Heat-sensitive genotype negatively correlates with long-chain fatty acids
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p002.s006: N22 and CN1 showed a higher percentage reduction of unsaturated fatty acids (C18:2 and C18:3), which are the major components of the thylakoid membrane, rendering the optimum thylakoid membrane fluidity and intactness of PSII complex. PMC9183074.p002.s007: Moreover, they exhibited sharp increases in long-chain fatty acids, particularly C22:1, while the heat sensitive IR64 and KDML105 showed significant reductions. PMC9183074.p002.s008: Dramatic increases in long-chain fatty acids may lead to cuticular wax synthesis which provides protective roles for heat tolerance. PMC9183074.p002.s009: Thus, the reduction in unsaturated fatty acid composition of the thylakoid membrane and dramatic increases in long-chain fatty acids may lead to high photosynthetic performance and an enhanced synthesis of cuticular wax which further provided additional protective roles for heat tolerance ability in rice.

events.event_label: Long-chain fatty acids promote cuticular wax synthesis contributing to heat tolerance
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p002.s006: N22 and CN1 showed a higher percentage reduction of unsaturated fatty acids (C18:2 and C18:3), which are the major components of the thylakoid membrane, rendering the optimum thylakoid membrane fluidity and intactness of PSII complex. PMC9183074.p002.s007: Moreover, they exhibited sharp increases in long-chain fatty acids, particularly C22:1, while the heat sensitive IR64 and KDML105 showed significant reductions. PMC9183074.p002.s008: Dramatic increases in long-chain fatty acids may lead to cuticular wax synthesis which provides protective roles for heat tolerance. PMC9183074.p002.s009: Thus, the reduction in unsaturated fatty acid composition of the thylakoid membrane and dramatic increases in long-chain fatty acids may lead to high photosynthetic performance and an enhanced synthesis of cuticular wax which further provided additional protective roles for heat tolerance ability in rice.

events.event_label: ROS induces chlorophyll degradation leading to leaf senescence
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p004.s006: HT affects PSII by significantly decreasing its function. PMC9183074.p004.s007: Several aspects of HT effects on PSII have been reported, including the disassociation of the PSII peripheral antenna complex from the core complex, inactivation and disassociation of oxygen-evolving PSII complexes, and inhibition of electron transport from QA to QB. PMC9183074.p004.s008: The photosynthetic carbon fixation is disrupted by temperatures above optimum for plant function. PMC9183074.p004.s009: This commonly coincides with chlorophyll degradation (caused by reactive oxygen species; ROS) resulting in rapid leaf senescence.

events.event_label: High temperature disrupts membrane fluidity and lipid membrane
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p007.s015: Previous reports in rice showed that HT led to significantly increased lipid peroxidation and electrolyte leakage, in cvs. PMC9183074.p007.s016: Dular, IR64 and KDML105 at seedling, and in cvs. PMC9183074.p007.s017: IR64 and KDML105 at the dough grain stage. PMC9183074.p007.s018: HT led to changes in lipid membrane structure and membrane fluidity relating to changes in lipid composition and saturation level. PMC9183074.p007.s019: Increased lipid saturation level was reported in creeping bentgrass exposed to high soil temperature (35 °C) or high ambient temperature (35 °C).

events.event_label: High temperature increases saturated fatty acid abundance
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p007.s018: HT led to changes in lipid membrane structure and membrane fluidity relating to changes in lipid composition and saturation level. PMC9183074.p007.s019: Increased lipid saturation level was reported in creeping bentgrass exposed to high soil temperature (35 °C) or high ambient temperature (35 °C). PMC9183074.p007.s020: In tall fescue turfgrass, increased lipid unsaturation was reported when exposed to HT (40/35 °C, 14/10 h), but in wheat under HT, there was a decline in the degree of lipid unsaturation. PMC9183074.p007.s021: Several reports pronounced the alteration of lipid composition upon heat stress such as the increase in degree of saturated fatty acids or decrease in unsaturated fatty acids. PMC9183074.p007.s022: Hu et al. proposed that an increase in unsaturated fatty acids enhanced lipid thermostability in a photosynthetic membrane and might contribute to heat tolerance in various species.

events.event_label: Heat stress increases saturated fatty acids and decreases unsaturated fatty acids
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p007.s018: HT led to changes in lipid membrane structure and membrane fluidity relating to changes in lipid composition and saturation level. PMC9183074.p007.s019: Increased lipid saturation level was reported in creeping bentgrass exposed to high soil temperature (35 °C) or high ambient temperature (35 °C). PMC9183074.p007.s020: In tall fescue turfgrass, increased lipid unsaturation was reported when exposed to HT (40/35 °C, 14/10 h), but in wheat under HT, there was a decline in the degree of lipid unsaturation. PMC9183074.p007.s021: Several reports pronounced the alteration of lipid composition upon heat stress such as the increase in degree of saturated fatty acids or decrease in unsaturated fatty acids. PMC9183074.p007.s022: Hu et al. proposed that an increase in unsaturated fatty acids enhanced lipid thermostability in a photosynthetic membrane and might contribute to heat tolerance in various species.

events.event_label: HT stress damages cellular membranes and increases EL
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p012.s001: All four rice genotypes under the control condition displayed good membrane stability as indicated by low EL values ranging from 3.24% to 4.16% (Figure 2A). PMC9183074.p012.s002: When subjected to HT stress, cellular membranes were damaged, and all rice genotypes showed significantly higher EL. PMC9183074.p012.s003: The membranes of the heat-sensitive check, IR64, was most seriously damaged, showing a nine-fold increase in EL, while those of N22 showed the highest integrity with only a 7.15% increase in EL. PMC9183074.p012.s004: Of the two Thai cultivars, CN1 maintained greater membrane stability with its EL value similar to that of N22.

events.event_label: HT decreases abundance of palmitic and stearic acids
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p017.s003: Only one saturated fatty acid, namely capric acid (C10:0), was found only in Thai rice, CN1 and KDML105. PMC9183074.p017.s004: Moreover, capric acid contents in HT-stressed CN1 and KDML105 exhibited significant increments. PMC9183074.p017.s005: Palmitic acid (C16:0) was found in the greatest amount under both control and HT conditions. PMC9183074.p017.s006: Palmitic acid and stearic acid markedly decreased with HT in all cultivars. PMC9183074.p017.s007: Three saturated fatty acids including arachidic acid, behenic acid, and lignoceric acid in N22, CN1 and IR64 were significantly increased after HT treatment, but in KDML105, the contents of these acids declined significantly.

events.event_label: HT decreases polyunsaturated fatty acids in seedlings
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p020.s003: Upon HT exposure, monounsaturated fatty acids significantly increased in N22 and IR64, but significantly decreased in Thai rice, CN1. PMC9183074.p020.s004: Polyunsaturated fatty acids in seedlings of all rice cultivars markedly decreased under HT. PMC9183074.p020.s005: Thai rice, CN1 exhibited the highest level of polyunsaturated fatty acid compared to other cultivars in both control and HT.

events.event_label: HT affects chloroplast and PSII
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p024.s003: HT can induce excessive reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation, resulting in cellular membrane injury, impaired photosynthetic mechanism and oxygen evolving complex associated with PSII, and inhibition of protein synthesis. PMC9183074.p024.s004: Plants can adapt to HT by changing the morphology and altering metabolism via signal transduction. PMC9183074.p024.s005: The chloroplast photosynthetic function is supposed to be the most HT-sensitive cellular function, and PSII is one of the most sensitive to HT. PMC9183074.p024.s006: In this study, PSII efficiency was determined by measuring the maximum quantum yield in the dark-adapted stage (Fv/Fm), which decreased significantly after exposure to HT in CN1, KDML105 and IR64 but remained unchanged in the heat-tolerant genotype, N22 (Figure 1). PMC9183074.p024.s007: Heat-induced reduction in Fv/Fm resulted from an increase in minimum fluorescence in the dark-adapted state (F0) which indicated the inhibition of electron transport and a reduction in maximum fluorescence in the dark-adapted state (Fm) that is caused by a dissociation of peripheral antenna complex of PSII from its core complex.

events.event_label: HT decreases PSII efficiency (Fv/Fm)
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p024.s003: HT can induce excessive reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation, resulting in cellular membrane injury, impaired photosynthetic mechanism and oxygen evolving complex associated with PSII, and inhibition of protein synthesis. PMC9183074.p024.s004: Plants can adapt to HT by changing the morphology and altering metabolism via signal transduction. PMC9183074.p024.s005: The chloroplast photosynthetic function is supposed to be the most HT-sensitive cellular function, and PSII is one of the most sensitive to HT. PMC9183074.p024.s006: In this study, PSII efficiency was determined by measuring the maximum quantum yield in the dark-adapted stage (Fv/Fm), which decreased significantly after exposure to HT in CN1, KDML105 and IR64 but remained unchanged in the heat-tolerant genotype, N22 (Figure 1). PMC9183074.p024.s007: Heat-induced reduction in Fv/Fm resulted from an increase in minimum fluorescence in the dark-adapted state (F0) which indicated the inhibition of electron transport and a reduction in maximum fluorescence in the dark-adapted state (Fm) that is caused by a dissociation of peripheral antenna complex of PSII from its core complex.

events.event_label: High temperature impairs PSII electron transport and antenna complex
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p004.s006: HT affects PSII by significantly decreasing its function. PMC9183074.p004.s007: Several aspects of HT effects on PSII have been reported, including the disassociation of the PSII peripheral antenna complex from the core complex, inactivation and disassociation of oxygen-evolving PSII complexes, and inhibition of electron transport from QA to QB. PMC9183074.p004.s008: The photosynthetic carbon fixation is disrupted by temperatures above optimum for plant function. PMC9183074.p004.s009: This commonly coincides with chlorophyll degradation (caused by reactive oxygen species; ROS) resulting in rapid leaf senescence.

events.event_label: HT reduces membrane thermostability and photosynthetic efficiency (Fv/Fm)
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p025.s006: Pansarakham et al. previously reported that MDA and EL of rice cvs. PMC9183074.p025.s007: IR64, KDML105, and PTT1 significantly increased after an exposure to 42 °C for 7 days while those of Dular and N22 remained unchanged. PMC9183074.p025.s008: This suggested that HT caused a reduction in membrane thermostability in IR64 and KDML105 and resulted in markedly decreased photosynthetic efficiency of PSII (Fv/Fm) (Figure 1). PMC9183074.p025.s009: Thylakoid membrane is the site where light harvesting pigments, PSII, PSI and electron carrier proteins are embedded. PMC9183074.p025.s010: Thus, the thylakoid membrane-increased fluidity due to HT resulted in weakness in the bonding between lipid and electron carrier protein molecules.

events.event_label: saturated fatty acids detected in rice seedlings
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p017.s001: A total of nine saturated fatty acids were identified from rice seedlings, and the content of each acid under the control and HT treatment are displayed in Table 1. PMC9183074.p017.s002: Eight saturated fatty acids, namely lauric acid (C12:0), myristic acid (C14:0), pentadecanoic acid (C15:0), palmitic acid (C16:0), stearic acid (C18:0), arachidic acid (C20:0), behenic acid (C22:0) and lignoceric acid (24:0), were found in seedlings of all four rice cultivars in both control and HT conditions. PMC9183074.p017.s003: Only one saturated fatty acid, namely capric acid (C10:0), was found only in Thai rice, CN1 and KDML105. PMC9183074.p017.s004: Moreover, capric acid contents in HT-stressed CN1 and KDML105 exhibited significant increments.

events.event_label: unsaturated fatty acids detected in rice seedlings under control and HT
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p019.s001: A total of nine unsaturated fatty acids were identified, and the contents under the control and HT treatment are shown in Table 2. PMC9183074.p019.s002: Six-unsaturated fatty acids including linoleic acid (C18:2), γ-Linolenic acid (C18:3n6), linolenic acid (C18:3), cis-5,8,11,14,17-eicosapentaenoic acid (C20:5), oleic acid (C18:1) and erucic acid (C22:1) were found in seedlings of all four rice cultivars under both conditions. PMC9183074.p019.s003: Cis-11,14-eicosadienoic acid (C20:2) which was present in the non-stress seedlings of all cultivars completely disappeared from all HT-stressed seedlings. PMC9183074.p019.s004: In the control seedlings, this acid was found in slightly greater amounts in the Thai rice, CN1 and KDML105.

events.event_label: Palmitic acid abundant under both control and HT conditions
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p017.s003: Only one saturated fatty acid, namely capric acid (C10:0), was found only in Thai rice, CN1 and KDML105. PMC9183074.p017.s004: Moreover, capric acid contents in HT-stressed CN1 and KDML105 exhibited significant increments. PMC9183074.p017.s005: Palmitic acid (C16:0) was found in the greatest amount under both control and HT conditions. PMC9183074.p017.s006: Palmitic acid and stearic acid markedly decreased with HT in all cultivars. PMC9183074.p017.s007: Three saturated fatty acids including arachidic acid, behenic acid, and lignoceric acid in N22, CN1 and IR64 were significantly increased after HT treatment, but in KDML105, the contents of these acids declined significantly.

events.event_label: unsaturated fatty acids detected in rice seedlings and rice
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p019.s001: A total of nine unsaturated fatty acids were identified, and the contents under the control and HT treatment are shown in Table 2. PMC9183074.p019.s002: Six-unsaturated fatty acids including linoleic acid (C18:2), γ-Linolenic acid (C18:3n6), linolenic acid (C18:3), cis-5,8,11,14,17-eicosapentaenoic acid (C20:5), oleic acid (C18:1) and erucic acid (C22:1) were found in seedlings of all four rice cultivars under both conditions. PMC9183074.p019.s003: Cis-11,14-eicosadienoic acid (C20:2) which was present in the non-stress seedlings of all cultivars completely disappeared from all HT-stressed seedlings. PMC9183074.p019.s004: In the control seedlings, this acid was found in slightly greater amounts in the Thai rice, CN1 and KDML105.

events.event_label: MGDG and DGDG are localized in thylakoid membrane
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p026.s009: The major types of lipids in the thylakoid membrane are galactolipids (monogalactosyldiacylglycerol, MGDG and digalactosyldiacylglycerol, DGDG), sulfolipids (sulfoquinovosyldiacylglycerol, SQDG), and phospholipids (phosphatidylglycerol, PG). PMC9183074.p026.s010: Of all lipids in thylakoids, MGDG and DGDG can reach 52% and 26%, respectively. PMC9183074.p026.s011: The ratio of MGDG to DGDG can indicate the photosynthetic membrane stability. PMC9183074.p026.s012: The major types of fatty acids that constitute lipids (MGDG, DGDG, SQDG and PG) in the plant thylakoid membrane are palmitic acid (C16:0), palmitoleic acid (C16:1), roughanic acid (C16:3), oleic acid (C18:1), linoleic acid (C18:2), and linolenic acid (C18:3). PMC9183074.p026.s013: Unsaturated fatty acids constitute 75% of total fatty acids in the thylakoid membrane, while the saturated fatty acid composition was approximately 25%.

events.event_label: Both unsaturated and saturated fatty acids are present in thylakoid membrane
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p026.s012: The major types of fatty acids that constitute lipids (MGDG, DGDG, SQDG and PG) in the plant thylakoid membrane are palmitic acid (C16:0), palmitoleic acid (C16:1), roughanic acid (C16:3), oleic acid (C18:1), linoleic acid (C18:2), and linolenic acid (C18:3). PMC9183074.p026.s013: Unsaturated fatty acids constitute 75% of total fatty acids in the thylakoid membrane, while the saturated fatty acid composition was approximately 25%. PMC9183074.p026.s014: The total saturated fatty acid content under HT remained unchanged in CN1 but significantly declined in KDML105 (Figure 4, Table 1). PMC9183074.p026.s015: CN1 also contained the highest, and KDML105 the lowest amount of saturated fatty acids under HT. PMC9183074.p026.s016: This suggested that, in CN1, lipid saturation level was well maintained under HT when associated with lower lipid peroxidation, a smaller increase in membrane fluidity, and lower EL (Figure 2).

events.event_label: Aromatic rice is associated with aroma and flavour
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p027.s012: Capric acid (C10:0) occurs in coconut oil, and it is used as an aromatic chemical in the manufacture of perfumes. PMC9183074.p027.s013: This suggested that Thai rice, CN1 and KDML105, especially KDML105 or Thai Jasmine rice, is an aromatic rice and has volatile aromatic compounds that make the aroma and flavour in the rice. PMC9183074.p027.s014: Thus, capric acid (C10:0) in Thai rice may involve aroma and flavour, not only 2AP.

events.event_label: capric acid associated with aroma and flavour
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p027.s012: Capric acid (C10:0) occurs in coconut oil, and it is used as an aromatic chemical in the manufacture of perfumes. PMC9183074.p027.s013: This suggested that Thai rice, CN1 and KDML105, especially KDML105 or Thai Jasmine rice, is an aromatic rice and has volatile aromatic compounds that make the aroma and flavour in the rice. PMC9183074.p027.s014: Thus, capric acid (C10:0) in Thai rice may involve aroma and flavour, not only 2AP.

events.event_label: HT increases electrolyte leakage and lipid peroxidation
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p126.s001: Effect of HT on electrolyte leakage (A) and lipid peroxidation (B) in four rice cultivars (N22, CN1, KDML105 and IR64). PMC9183074.p126.s002: Each bar represents mean ± SE (n = 3–4). PMC9183074.p126.s003: Asterisk (*) indicates significant difference between control and HT in each rice cultivar at p ≤ 0.05 by t test. PMC9183074.p126.s004: Different capital letters indicate significant difference of control group in different rice cultivars at p ≤ 0.05 by ANOVA and DMRT, and small letters indicate significant difference of HT in different rice cultivars at p ≤ 0.05 by ANOVA and DMRT.

events.event_label: HT changes abundance of fatty acids
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p129.s001: Effect of HT on mono- and polyunsaturated fatty acids in four rice cultivars (N22, CN1, KDML105 and IR64). PMC9183074.p129.s002: Each bar represents mean ± SE (n = 3–4). PMC9183074.p129.s003: Asterisk (*) indicates significant difference between control and HT in mono- or poly unsaturated fatty acid and in each rice cultivar at p ≤ 0.05 by t test. PMC9183074.p129.s004: Different capital letters indicate significant difference of control group in different rice cultivars at p ≤ 0.05 by ANOVA and DMRT, and small letters indicate significant difference of HT in different rice cultivars at p ≤ 0.05 by ANOVA and DMRT.

events.event_label: HT increases membrane fluidity
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p006.s001: The fluidity of lipid membrane is important for membrane function that depends on the interaction between lipid and protein compositions. PMC9183074.p006.s002: The increase in membrane fluidity resulted from an HT-induced higher degree of lipid unsaturation. PMC9183074.p006.s003: Thus, to decrease membrane fluidity under HT, lipid unsaturations could be decreased by downregulating the fatty acid desaturase (FAD) gene. PMC9183074.p006.s004: Maintaining the optimum composition of unsaturated fatty acids is important in determining cellular membrane fluidity under HT.

events.event_label: HT stress reduces PSII efficiency (Fv/Fm)

participants.label: maximum quantum yield of PSII efficiency

events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p010.s001: Rice plants of all four cultivars in the control condition had the maximum quantum yield of PSII efficiency in the dark-adapted state (Fv/Fm) values equal to or higher than 0.80, indicating the absence of damage to PSII, with CN1 showing the highest value at 0.822 (Figure 1). PMC9183074.p010.s002: However, when subjected to HT stress, Fv/Fm significantly decreased in IR64 (the heat sensitive check), KDML105 and CN1. PMC9183074.p010.s003: In contrast, the heat-tolerant genotype (N22) maintained a high Fv/Fm value (approximately 0.806). PMC9183074.p010.s004: Heat stressed CN1 still had relatively high Fv/Fm that was slightly higher than N22.

events.event_label: HT increases palmitoleic acid
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p019.s003: Cis-11,14-eicosadienoic acid (C20:2) which was present in the non-stress seedlings of all cultivars completely disappeared from all HT-stressed seedlings. PMC9183074.p019.s004: In the control seedlings, this acid was found in slightly greater amounts in the Thai rice, CN1 and KDML105. PMC9183074.p019.s005: In the non-stress plants, palmitoleic acid (C16:1) was present in N22 and IR64 but absent from CN1 and KDML105. PMC9183074.p019.s006: Upon HT stress, the content of this acid significantly increased in all cultivars. PMC9183074.p019.s007: The content of palmitoleic acid in the HT plants of CN1 and KDML105 was present several folds higher than those in N22 and IR64.

events.event_label: Lipid changes affect thylakoid membrane, photosynthetic performance, and heat tolerance
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p002.s006: N22 and CN1 showed a higher percentage reduction of unsaturated fatty acids (C18:2 and C18:3), which are the major components of the thylakoid membrane, rendering the optimum thylakoid membrane fluidity and intactness of PSII complex. PMC9183074.p002.s007: Moreover, they exhibited sharp increases in long-chain fatty acids, particularly C22:1, while the heat sensitive IR64 and KDML105 showed significant reductions. PMC9183074.p002.s008: Dramatic increases in long-chain fatty acids may lead to cuticular wax synthesis which provides protective roles for heat tolerance. PMC9183074.p002.s009: Thus, the reduction in unsaturated fatty acid composition of the thylakoid membrane and dramatic increases in long-chain fatty acids may lead to high photosynthetic performance and an enhanced synthesis of cuticular wax which further provided additional protective roles for heat tolerance ability in rice.

events.event_label: HT decreases C18:3 and increases C16:0 and saturated fatty acids
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p026.s024: A decrease in unsaturation or increase in saturation of fatty acids in membrane lipids was known to help maintain appropriate membrane fluidity at high temperature. PMC9183074.p026.s025: Similar to our results, HT treatment in Brassica carinata resulted in remodelling of leaf lipidome manifested by decreases in unsaturated levels of membrane lipids, especially C18:3, and increases in saturated fatty acids such as C16:0. PMC9183074.p026.s026: These authors proposed that plants can modulate the level of unsaturation of membrane lipids by decreasing the activity of FAD3, FAD7 and FAD8, which catalyse the formation of C18:3, or by increasing the activity of FAD2 and fatty acid synthase (FAS), which catalyse the formation of C18:2 or C16:0, respectively. PMC9183074.p026.s027: The time of exposure and the level of temperature elicited different responses in relation to fatty acid changes, which varies depending on plant species. PMC9183074.p026.s028: In contrast to this study, Wang et al. reported that for temperate species such as Arabidopsis, heat shock at 38 °C for 6 h did not induce any changes in unsaturated fatty acids but prolonged treatment at warm temperature (28 °C, 7 d), resulting in an enrichment of these acids to achieve an optimum level of membrane fluidity.

events.event_label: MDA and EL levels correlate positively with HT and negatively with membrane thermostability
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p025.s003: HT can induce an increase in membrane fluidity and oxidative burst, leading to lipid peroxidation resulting in malondialdehyde production (MDA) and other changes in lipid membrane structure, which cause membrane injury, electrolyte leakage (EL) and cell death. PMC9183074.p025.s004: MDA content and EL are sufficient indicators of membrane thermostability, and they can be considered directly related to the degree of plant damage by HT. PMC9183074.p025.s005: In this study, CN1 and N22 exhibited slightly decreased membrane thermostability, as indicated by a small increase in EL and MDA content (Figure 2), but IR64 and aromatic Thai rice KDML105 displayed a sharp increase in EL and MDA content under HT (Figure 2). PMC9183074.p025.s006: Pansarakham et al. previously reported that MDA and EL of rice cvs. PMC9183074.p025.s007: IR64, KDML105, and PTT1 significantly increased after an exposure to 42 °C for 7 days while those of Dular and N22 remained unchanged.

events.event_label: HT increases EL and MDA, which negatively correlate with membrane thermostability

events.event_label: Aromatic rice and capric acid are associated with aroma and flavour

events.event_label: HT reduces unsaturated fatty acids, affecting membrane fluidity and stability
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p043.s001: HT caused differential alterations in fatty acid profiles, especially those comprising the thylakoid membrane in rice cultivars differing in heat tolerance. PMC9183074.p043.s002: In the case of N22 and CN1, their leaves slightly decreased the degree of lipid thylakoid membrane composition in the amount of palmitic acid (C16:0), linolenic acid (C18:2), γ-linolenic acid (C18:3n6) and linolenic acid (C18:3) and increased the amount of palmitoleic acid (C16:1). PMC9183074.p043.s003: The greater reduction in unsaturated fatty acids under HT, especially in N22, was related to maintenance of optimum thylakoid membrane fluidity and increased membrane stability. PMC9183074.p043.s004: Moreover, in N22 and CN1, long-chain fatty acids, which serve as precursors of cuticular wax synthesis, namely arachidic acid (C20:0), behenic acid (C22:0), lignoceric acid (24:0) and erucic acid (C22:1), also increased.

events.event_label: HT induces membrane damage: increased fluidity, lipid peroxidation, MDA, and electrolyte leakage
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p025.s001: The cellular membrane is the most sensitive structure to be affected by HT. PMC9183074.p025.s002: The thylakoid membrane, where the photosynthetic light reaction occurs, is also highly thermal sensitive. PMC9183074.p025.s003: HT can induce an increase in membrane fluidity and oxidative burst, leading to lipid peroxidation resulting in malondialdehyde production (MDA) and other changes in lipid membrane structure, which cause membrane injury, electrolyte leakage (EL) and cell death. PMC9183074.p025.s004: MDA content and EL are sufficient indicators of membrane thermostability, and they can be considered directly related to the degree of plant damage by HT.

events.event_label: FAD3/7/8 catalyze C18:3, FAD2 catalyzes C18:2, and FAS catalyzes C16:0
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p026.s024: A decrease in unsaturation or increase in saturation of fatty acids in membrane lipids was known to help maintain appropriate membrane fluidity at high temperature. PMC9183074.p026.s025: Similar to our results, HT treatment in Brassica carinata resulted in remodelling of leaf lipidome manifested by decreases in unsaturated levels of membrane lipids, especially C18:3, and increases in saturated fatty acids such as C16:0. PMC9183074.p026.s026: These authors proposed that plants can modulate the level of unsaturation of membrane lipids by decreasing the activity of FAD3, FAD7 and FAD8, which catalyse the formation of C18:3, or by increasing the activity of FAD2 and fatty acid synthase (FAS), which catalyse the formation of C18:2 or C16:0, respectively. PMC9183074.p026.s027: The time of exposure and the level of temperature elicited different responses in relation to fatty acid changes, which varies depending on plant species. PMC9183074.p026.s028: In contrast to this study, Wang et al. reported that for temperate species such as Arabidopsis, heat shock at 38 °C for 6 h did not induce any changes in unsaturated fatty acids but prolonged treatment at warm temperature (28 °C, 7 d), resulting in an enrichment of these acids to achieve an optimum level of membrane fluidity.

PMC9183074.p002.s007: Moreover, they exhibited sharp increases in long-chain fatty acids, particularly C22:1, while the heat sensitive IR64 and KDML105 showed significant reductions.

events.event_label: HT induces ROS accumulation and impairs photosynthetic efficiency
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p024.s001: High temperature (HT) is harmful to plant growth and survival. PMC9183074.p024.s002: It decreases photosynthetic efficiency and yield. PMC9183074.p024.s004: Plants can adapt to HT by changing the morphology and altering metabolism via signal transduction.

events.event_label: Thylakoid membrane contains MGDG, DGDG, SQDG, PG; palmitic, roughanic, oleic, and linolenic acids are precursors of MGDG/DGDG
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p026.s009: The major types of lipids in the thylakoid membrane are galactolipids (monogalactosyldiacylglycerol, MGDG and digalactosyldiacylglycerol, DGDG), sulfolipids (sulfoquinovosyldiacylglycerol, SQDG), and phospholipids (phosphatidylglycerol, PG). PMC9183074.p026.s011: The ratio of MGDG to DGDG can indicate the photosynthetic membrane stability. PMC9183074.p026.s012: The major types of fatty acids that constitute lipids (MGDG, DGDG, SQDG and PG) in the plant thylakoid membrane are palmitic acid (C16:0), palmitoleic acid (C16:1), roughanic acid (C16:3), oleic acid (C18:1), linoleic acid (C18:2), and linolenic acid (C18:3). PMC9183074.p026.s013: Unsaturated fatty acids constitute 75% of total fatty acids in the thylakoid membrane, while the saturated fatty acid composition was approximately 25%.

events.event_label: C18:2 influences membrane fluidity and PSII function in thylakoid membrane
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p002.s003: Twenty-eight-day-old seedlings of two Thai rice cultivars (CN1 and KDML105), a standard heat tolerance (N22), and a heat sensitive (IR64) rice genotype were treated at 42 °C for 7 days. PMC9183074.p002.s004: Under HT, N22 showed the highest heat tolerance displaying the lowest increase in electrolyte leakage (EL), no increments in malondialdehyde (MDA) and stable maximum quantum yield of PSII efficiency (Fv/Fm). PMC9183074.p002.s005: Compared to KDML105 and IR64, CN1 was more tolerant of HT, showing a lower increase in EL and MDA, and less reduction in Fv/Fm. PMC9183074.p002.s006: N22 and CN1 showed a higher percentage reduction of unsaturated fatty acids (C18:2 and C18:3), which are the major components of the thylakoid membrane, rendering the optimum thylakoid membrane fluidity and intactness of PSII complex. PMC9183074.p002.s007: Moreover, they exhibited sharp increases in long-chain fatty acids, particularly C22:1, while the heat sensitive IR64 and KDML105 showed significant reductions.

events.event_label: HT damages cellular membranes increasing electrolyte leakage
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p012.s001: All four rice genotypes under the control condition displayed good membrane stability as indicated by low EL values ranging from 3.24% to 4.16% (Figure 2A). PMC9183074.p012.s002: When subjected to HT stress, cellular membranes were damaged, and all rice genotypes showed significantly higher EL. PMC9183074.p012.s003: The membranes of the heat-sensitive check, IR64, was most seriously damaged, showing a nine-fold increase in EL, while those of N22 showed the highest integrity with only a 7.15% increase in EL. PMC9183074.p012.s004: Of the two Thai cultivars, CN1 maintained greater membrane stability with its EL value similar to that of N22.

events.event_label: HT reduces photosynthetic efficiency
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p024.s001: High temperature (HT) is harmful to plant growth and survival. PMC9183074.p024.s002: It decreases photosynthetic efficiency and yield. PMC9183074.p024.s003: HT can induce excessive reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation, resulting in cellular membrane injury, impaired photosynthetic mechanism and oxygen evolving complex associated with PSII, and inhibition of protein synthesis. PMC9183074.p024.s004: Plants can adapt to HT by changing the morphology and altering metabolism via signal transduction. PMC9183074.p024.s005: The chloroplast photosynthetic function is supposed to be the most HT-sensitive cellular function, and PSII is one of the most sensitive to HT. PMC9183074.p024.s006: In this study, PSII efficiency was determined by measuring the maximum quantum yield in the dark-adapted stage (Fv/Fm), which decreased significantly after exposure to HT in CN1, KDML105 and IR64 but remained unchanged in the heat-tolerant genotype, N22 (Figure 1). PMC9183074.p024.s007: Heat-induced reduction in Fv/Fm resulted from an increase in minimum fluorescence in the dark-adapted state (F0) which indicated the inhibition of electron transport and a reduction in maximum fluorescence in the dark-adapted state (Fm) that is caused by a dissociation of peripheral antenna complex of PSII from its core complex.

events.event_label: unsaturated fatty acids detected in rice seedlings
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p019.s001: A total of nine unsaturated fatty acids were identified, and the contents under the control and HT treatment are shown in Table 2. PMC9183074.p019.s002: Six-unsaturated fatty acids including linoleic acid (C18:2), γ-Linolenic acid (C18:3n6), linolenic acid (C18:3), cis-5,8,11,14,17-eicosapentaenoic acid (C20:5), oleic acid (C18:1) and erucic acid (C22:1) were found in seedlings of all four rice cultivars under both conditions. PMC9183074.p019.s003: Cis-11,14-eicosadienoic acid (C20:2) which was present in the non-stress seedlings of all cultivars completely disappeared from all HT-stressed seedlings. PMC9183074.p019.s004: In the control seedlings, this acid was found in slightly greater amounts in the Thai rice, CN1 and KDML105.

events.event_label: MGDG, DGDG, SQDG and PG are detected in thylakoid membrane
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p026.s009: The major types of lipids in the thylakoid membrane are galactolipids (monogalactosyldiacylglycerol, MGDG and digalactosyldiacylglycerol, DGDG), sulfolipids (sulfoquinovosyldiacylglycerol, SQDG), and phospholipids (phosphatidylglycerol, PG). PMC9183074.p026.s010: Of all lipids in thylakoids, MGDG and DGDG can reach 52% and 26%, respectively. PMC9183074.p026.s011: The ratio of MGDG to DGDG can indicate the photosynthetic membrane stability. PMC9183074.p026.s012: The major types of fatty acids that constitute lipids (MGDG, DGDG, SQDG and PG) in the plant thylakoid membrane are palmitic acid (C16:0), palmitoleic acid (C16:1), roughanic acid (C16:3), oleic acid (C18:1), linoleic acid (C18:2), and linolenic acid (C18:3). PMC9183074.p026.s013: Unsaturated fatty acids constitute 75% of total fatty acids in the thylakoid membrane, while the saturated fatty acid composition was approximately 25%.

events.event_label: HT impairs PSII function
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p004.s003: It also causes a loss in membrane integrity, aging in plant cells, inhibition of plant growth and cell death. PMC9183074.p004.s004: A temperature greater than 40 °C affects many crops by causing thermal injury, inhibiting photosynthesis and changing protein structure and function such as protein unfolding, denaturation, aggregation and inactivation of enzyme reactions. PMC9183074.p004.s005: The first and highly sensitive photosynthetic site which is affected by HT is photosystem II (PSII). PMC9183074.p004.s006: HT affects PSII by significantly decreasing its function. PMC9183074.p004.s007: Several aspects of HT effects on PSII have been reported, including the disassociation of the PSII peripheral antenna complex from the core complex, inactivation and disassociation of oxygen-evolving PSII complexes, and inhibition of electron transport from QA to QB. PMC9183074.p004.s008: The photosynthetic carbon fixation is disrupted by temperatures above optimum for plant function. PMC9183074.p004.s009: This commonly coincides with chlorophyll degradation (caused by reactive oxygen species; ROS) resulting in rapid leaf senescence.

events.event_label: FAD enzymes catalyze C18:3 formation
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p026.s024: A decrease in unsaturation or increase in saturation of fatty acids in membrane lipids was known to help maintain appropriate membrane fluidity at high temperature. PMC9183074.p026.s025: Similar to our results, HT treatment in Brassica carinata resulted in remodelling of leaf lipidome manifested by decreases in unsaturated levels of membrane lipids, especially C18:3, and increases in saturated fatty acids such as C16:0. PMC9183074.p026.s026: These authors proposed that plants can modulate the level of unsaturation of membrane lipids by decreasing the activity of FAD3, FAD7 and FAD8, which catalyse the formation of C18:3, or by increasing the activity of FAD2 and fatty acid synthase (FAS), which catalyse the formation of C18:2 or C16:0, respectively. PMC9183074.p026.s027: The time of exposure and the level of temperature elicited different responses in relation to fatty acid changes, which varies depending on plant species. PMC9183074.p026.s028: In contrast to this study, Wang et al. reported that for temperate species such as Arabidopsis, heat shock at 38 °C for 6 h did not induce any changes in unsaturated fatty acids but prolonged treatment at warm temperature (28 °C, 7 d), resulting in an enrichment of these acids to achieve an optimum level of membrane fluidity.

events.event_label: HT induces membrane damage
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p025.s001: The cellular membrane is the most sensitive structure to be affected by HT. PMC9183074.p025.s002: The thylakoid membrane, where the photosynthetic light reaction occurs, is also highly thermal sensitive. PMC9183074.p025.s003: HT can induce an increase in membrane fluidity and oxidative burst, leading to lipid peroxidation resulting in malondialdehyde production (MDA) and other changes in lipid membrane structure, which cause membrane injury, electrolyte leakage (EL) and cell death. PMC9183074.p025.s004: MDA content and EL are sufficient indicators of membrane thermostability, and they can be considered directly related to the degree of plant damage by HT. PMC9183074.p025.s006: Pansarakham et al. previously reported that MDA and EL of rice cvs. PMC9183074.p025.s007: IR64, KDML105, and PTT1 significantly increased after an exposure to 42 °C for 7 days while those of Dular and N22 remained unchanged. PMC9183074.p025.s008: This suggested that HT caused a reduction in membrane thermostability in IR64 and KDML105 and resulted in markedly decreased photosynthetic efficiency of PSII (Fv/Fm) (Figure 1). PMC9183074.p025.s009: Thylakoid membrane is the site where light harvesting pigments, PSII, PSI and electron carrier proteins are embedded. PMC9183074.p025.s010: Thus, the thylakoid membrane-increased fluidity due to HT resulted in weakness in the bonding between lipid and electron carrier protein molecules.

events.event_label: Thylakoid membrane contains MGDG, DGDG, SQDG, PG and saturated fatty acids
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p026.s009: The major types of lipids in the thylakoid membrane are galactolipids (monogalactosyldiacylglycerol, MGDG and digalactosyldiacylglycerol, DGDG), sulfolipids (sulfoquinovosyldiacylglycerol, SQDG), and phospholipids (phosphatidylglycerol, PG). PMC9183074.p026.s010: Of all lipids in thylakoids, MGDG and DGDG can reach 52% and 26%, respectively. PMC9183074.p026.s011: The ratio of MGDG to DGDG can indicate the photosynthetic membrane stability. PMC9183074.p026.s012: The major types of fatty acids that constitute lipids (MGDG, DGDG, SQDG and PG) in the plant thylakoid membrane are palmitic acid (C16:0), palmitoleic acid (C16:1), roughanic acid (C16:3), oleic acid (C18:1), linoleic acid (C18:2), and linolenic acid (C18:3). PMC9183074.p026.s013: Unsaturated fatty acids constitute 75% of total fatty acids in the thylakoid membrane, while the saturated fatty acid composition was approximately 25%. PMC9183074.p026.s014: The total saturated fatty acid content under HT remained unchanged in CN1 but significantly declined in KDML105 (Figure 4, Table 1). PMC9183074.p026.s015: CN1 also contained the highest, and KDML105 the lowest amount of saturated fatty acids under HT. PMC9183074.p026.s016: This suggested that, in CN1, lipid saturation level was well maintained under HT when associated with lower lipid peroxidation, a smaller increase in membrane fluidity, and lower EL (Figure 2).

events.event_label: HT decreases abundance of γ-Linolenic acid and Linolenic acid
HT increases behenic acid in seedlings
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p017.s001: A total of nine saturated fatty acids were identified from rice seedlings, and the content of each acid under the control and HT treatment are displayed in Table 1. PMC9183074.p017.s002: Eight saturated fatty acids, namely lauric acid (C12:0), myristic acid (C14:0), pentadecanoic acid (C15:0), palmitic acid (C16:0), stearic acid (C18:0), arachidic acid (C20:0), behenic acid (C22:0) and lignoceric acid (24:0), were found in seedlings of all four rice cultivars in both control and HT conditions. PMC9183074.p017.s003: Only one saturated fatty acid, namely capric acid (C10:0), was found only in Thai rice, CN1 and KDML105. PMC9183074.p017.s004: Moreover, capric acid contents in HT-stressed CN1 and KDML105 exhibited significant increments. PMC9183074.p017.s006: Palmitic acid and stearic acid markedly decreased with HT in all cultivars. PMC9183074.p017.s007: Three saturated fatty acids including arachidic acid, behenic acid, and lignoceric acid in N22, CN1 and IR64 were significantly increased after HT treatment, but in KDML105, the contents of these acids declined significantly.

events.event_label: Long-chain fatty acids promote cuticular wax synthesis and photosynthetic performance
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p002.s006: N22 and CN1 showed a higher percentage reduction of unsaturated fatty acids (C18:2 and C18:3), which are the major components of the thylakoid membrane, rendering the optimum thylakoid membrane fluidity and intactness of PSII complex. PMC9183074.p002.s007: Moreover, they exhibited sharp increases in long-chain fatty acids, particularly C22:1, while the heat sensitive IR64 and KDML105 showed significant reductions. PMC9183074.p002.s008: Dramatic increases in long-chain fatty acids may lead to cuticular wax synthesis which provides protective roles for heat tolerance. PMC9183074.p002.s009: Thus, the reduction in unsaturated fatty acid composition of the thylakoid membrane and dramatic increases in long-chain fatty acids may lead to high photosynthetic performance and an enhanced synthesis of cuticular wax which further provided additional protective roles for heat tolerance ability in rice.

events.event_label: HT increases MDA, which correlates negatively with membrane thermostability
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p025.s003: HT can induce an increase in membrane fluidity and oxidative burst, leading to lipid peroxidation resulting in malondialdehyde production (MDA) and other changes in lipid membrane structure, which cause membrane injury, electrolyte leakage (EL) and cell death. events.event_label: HT decreases abundance of unsaturated fatty acids
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p043.s001: HT caused differential alterations in fatty acid profiles, especially those comprising the thylakoid membrane in rice cultivars differing in heat tolerance. PMC9183074.p043.s002: In the case of N22 and CN1, their leaves slightly decreased the degree of lipid thylakoid membrane composition in the amount of palmitic acid (C16:0), linolenic acid (C18:2), γ-linolenic acid (C18:3n6) and linolenic acid (C18:3) and increased the amount of palmitoleic acid (C16:1). PMC9183074.p043.s003: The greater reduction in unsaturated fatty acids under HT, especially in N22, was related to maintenance of optimum thylakoid membrane fluidity and increased membrane stability. PMC9183074.p043.s004: Moreover, in N22 and CN1, long-chain fatty acids, which serve as precursors of cuticular wax synthesis, namely arachidic acid (C20:0), behenic acid (C22:0), lignoceric acid (24:0) and erucic acid (C22:1), also increased.

events.event_label: HT alters membrane fluidity via lipid unsaturation changes
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p006.s001: The fluidity of lipid membrane is important for membrane function that depends on the interaction between lipid and protein compositions. PMC9183074.p006.s002: The increase in membrane fluidity resulted from an HT-induced higher degree of lipid unsaturation. PMC9183074.p006.s003: Thus, to decrease membrane fluidity under HT, lipid unsaturations could be decreased by downregulating the fatty acid desaturase (FAD) gene. PMC9183074.p006.s004: Maintaining the optimum composition of unsaturated fatty acids is important in determining cellular membrane fluidity under HT. PMC9183074.p006.s009: A heat-tolerant wheat genotype showed higher amounts of saturated lipids and a decrease in the amount of unsaturated lipids. PMC9183074.p006.s010: Similarly, Brassica carinata cv. PMC9183074.p006.s011: Avanza 641 responded to HT by decreasing the level of unsaturation in membrane lipid, especially in unsaturated linolenic acid (18:3) to maintain optimal membrane fluidity. PMC9183074.p006.s012: Moreover, soybean and peanut also responded to HT by decreasing lipid unsaturation levels to maintain membrane fluidity, and the magnitudes of reduction were greater for heat-tolerant than for heat-sensitive genotypes. PMC9183074.p006.s013: In contrast, some experiments demonstrated that higher degrees of unsaturated fatty acids can maintain membrane fluidity under HT.

events.event_label: heat stress increases saturated fatty acids
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p007.s018: HT led to changes in lipid membrane structure and membrane fluidity relating to changes in lipid composition and saturation level. PMC9183074.p007.s019: Increased lipid saturation level was reported in creeping bentgrass exposed to high soil temperature (35 °C) or high ambient temperature (35 °C). PMC9183074.p007.s020: In tall fescue turfgrass, increased lipid unsaturation was reported when exposed to HT (40/35 °C, 14/10 h), but in wheat under HT, there was a decline in the degree of lipid unsaturation. PMC9183074.p007.s021: Several reports pronounced the alteration of lipid composition upon heat stress such as the increase in degree of saturated fatty acids or decrease in unsaturated fatty acids. PMC9183074.p007.s022: Hu et al. proposed that an increase in unsaturated fatty acids enhanced lipid thermostability in a photosynthetic membrane and might contribute to heat tolerance in various species.

events.event_label: HT induces multiple membrane and photosynthetic alterations
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p007.s009: A lower maximum quantum yield of PSII efficiency (Fv/Fm) was found in tall fescue grass exposed to 35/30 °C (day/night) for 20 days, and in spinach stressed at 50 °C. PMC9183074.p007.s010: In rice, significantly reduced Fv/Fm was reported when plants were subjected to heat stress at 40–55 °C for 30 min at seedling, 35 and 40 °C at tillering, 45 °C for 30 min at heading and 42 °C for 30 min at dough grain stage. PMC9183074.p007.s011: The decline in photosynthetic pigments as affected by HT was found in tall fescue grass, in seedlings of rice cv. PMC9183074.p007.s012: Riceberry, and in rice cvs. PMC9183074.p007.s013: IR64 and KDML105 at the dough grain stage. PMC9183074.p007.s015: Previous reports in rice showed that HT led to significantly increased lipid peroxidation and electrolyte leakage, in cvs. PMC9183074.p007.s016: Dular, IR64 and KDML105 at seedling, and in cvs. PMC9183074.p007.s017: IR64 and KDML105 at the dough grain stage. PMC9183074.p007.s018: HT led to changes in lipid membrane structure and membrane fluidity relating to changes in lipid composition and saturation level. PMC9183074.p007.s019: Increased lipid saturation level was reported in creeping bentgrass exposed to high soil temperature (35 °C) or high ambient temperature (35 °C). PMC9183074.p007.s021: Several reports pronounced the alteration of lipid composition upon heat stress such as the increase in degree of saturated fatty acids or decrease in unsaturated fatty acids. PMC9183074.p007.s022: Hu et al. proposed that an increase in unsaturated fatty acids enhanced lipid thermostability in a photosynthetic membrane and might contribute to heat tolerance in various species. PMC9183074.p007.s023: Therefore, the experimental results inferring the relationship between lipid composition (degree of fatty acid saturation/unsaturation) and heat tolerance in various species are still inconclusive. PMC9183074.p007.s024: Thus, this research studied the HT responses of two Thai rice cultivars compared to a standard heat tolerance and a heat-sensitive rice genotype, in terms of physiological parameters and leaf fatty acid composition changes.

PMC9183074.p019.s005: In the non-stress plants, palmitoleic acid (C16:1) was present in N22 and IR64 but absent from CN1 and KDML105. PMC9183074.p019.s006: Upon HT stress, the content of this acid significantly increased in all cultivars. PMC9183074.p019.s007: The content of palmitoleic acid in the HT plants of CN1 and KDML105 was present several folds higher than those in N22 and IR64.

events.event_label: HT triggers lipid peroxidation producing MDA, affecting membrane stability and photosynthetic efficiency
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p025.s001: The cellular membrane is the most sensitive structure to be affected by HT. PMC9183074.p025.s002: The thylakoid membrane, where the photosynthetic light reaction occurs, is also highly thermal sensitive. PMC9183074.p025.s003: HT can induce an increase in membrane fluidity and oxidative burst, leading to lipid peroxidation resulting in malondialdehyde production (MDA) and other changes in lipid membrane structure, which cause membrane injury, electrolyte leakage (EL) and cell death. PMC9183074.p025.s004: MDA content and EL are sufficient indicators of membrane thermostability, and they can be considered directly related to the degree of plant damage by HT. PMC9183074.p025.s005: In this study, CN1 and N22 exhibited slightly decreased membrane thermostability, as indicated by a small increase in EL and MDA content (Figure 2), but IR64 and aromatic Thai rice KDML105 displayed a sharp increase in EL and MDA content under HT (Figure 2). PMC9183074.p025.s006: Pansarakham et al. previously reported that MDA and EL of rice cvs. PMC9183074.p025.s007: IR64, KDML105, and PTT1 significantly increased after an exposure to 42 °C for 7 days while those of Dular and N22 remained unchanged. PMC9183074.p025.s008: This suggested that HT caused a reduction in membrane thermostability in IR64 and KDML105 and resulted in markedly decreased photosynthetic efficiency of PSII (Fv/Fm) (Figure 1). PMC9183074.p025.s009: Thylakoid membrane is the site where light harvesting pigments, PSII, PSI and electron carrier proteins are embedded. PMC9183074.p025.s010: Thus, the thylakoid membrane-increased fluidity due to HT resulted in weakness in the bonding between lipid and electron carrier protein molecules.

events.event_label: fatty acids are precursors of DGDG in thylakoid membrane
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p026.s009: The major types of lipids in the thylakoid membrane are galactolipids (monogalactosyldiacylglycerol, MGDG and digalactosyldiacylglycerol, DGDG), sulfolipids (sulfoquinovosyldiacylglycerol, SQDG), and phospholipids (phosphatidylglycerol, PG). PMC9183074.p026.s010: Of all lipids in thylakoids, MGDG and DGDG can reach 52% and 26%, respectively. PMC9183074.p026.s011: The ratio of MGDG to DGDG can indicate the photosynthetic membrane stability. PMC9183074.p026.s012: The major types of fatty acids that constitute lipids (MGDG, DGDG, SQDG and PG) in the plant thylakoid membrane are palmitic acid (C16:0), palmitoleic acid (C16:1), roughanic acid (C16:3), oleic acid (C18:1), linoleic acid (C18:2), and linolenic acid (C18:3). PMC9183074.p026.s013: Unsaturated fatty acids constitute 75% of total fatty acids in the thylakoid membrane, while the saturated fatty acid composition was approximately 25%.

events.event_label: HT impairs PSII function
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p004.s003: It also causes a loss in membrane integrity, aging in plant cells, inhibition of plant growth and cell death. PMC9183074.p004.s004: A temperature greater than 40 °C affects many crops by causing thermal injury, inhibiting photosynthesis and changing protein structure and function such as protein unfolding, denaturation, aggregation and inactivation of enzyme reactions. PMC9183074.p004.s005: The first and highly sensitive photosynthetic site which is affected by HT is photosystem II (PSII). PMC9183074.p004.s006: HT affects PSII by significantly decreasing its function. PMC9183074.p004.s007: Several aspects of HT effects on PSII have been reported, including the disassociation of the PSII peripheral antenna complex from the core complex, inactivation and disassociation of oxygen-evolving PSII complexes, and inhibition of electron transport from QA to QB. PMC9183074.p004.s008: The photosynthetic carbon fixation is disrupted by temperatures above optimum for plant function. PMC9183074.p004.s009: This commonly coincides with chlorophyll degradation (caused by reactive oxygen species; ROS) resulting in rapid leaf senescence.

PMC9183074.p017.s004: Moreover, capric acid contents in HT-stressed CN1 and KDML105 exhibited significant increments.

events.event_label: ROS impairs photosynthetic mechanism and oxygen evolving complex
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p024.s001: High temperature (HT) is harmful to plant growth and survival. PMC9183074.p024.s002: It decreases photosynthetic efficiency and yield. PMC9183074.p024.s003: HT can induce excessive reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation, resulting in cellular membrane injury, impaired photosynthetic mechanism and oxygen evolving complex associated with PSII, and inhibition of protein synthesis. PMC9183074.p024.s004: Plants can adapt to HT by changing the morphology and altering metabolism via signal transduction.

events.event_label: HT impairs PSII function
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p004.s003: It also causes a loss in membrane integrity, aging in plant cells, inhibition of plant growth and cell death. PMC9183074.p004.s004: A temperature greater than 40 °C affects many crops by causing thermal injury, inhibiting photosynthesis and changing protein structure and function such as protein unfolding, denaturation, aggregation and inactivation of enzyme reactions. PMC9183074.p004.s005: The first and highly sensitive photosynthetic site which is affected by HT is photosystem II (PSII). PMC9183074.p004.s006: HT affects PSII by significantly decreasing its function. PMC9183074.p004.s007: Several aspects of HT effects on PSII have been reported, including the disassociation of the PSII peripheral antenna complex from the core complex, inactivation and disassociation of oxygen-evolving PSII complexes, and inhibition of electron transport from QA to QB. PMC9183074.p004.s008: The photosynthetic carbon fixation is disrupted by temperatures above optimum for plant function. PMC9183074.p004.s009: This commonly coincides with chlorophyll degradation (caused by reactive oxygen species; ROS) resulting in rapid leaf senescence.

events.event_label: MDA negatively correlates with membrane thermostability
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p025.s003: HT can induce an increase in membrane fluidity and oxidative burst, leading to lipid peroxidation resulting in malondialdehyde production (MDA) and other changes in lipid membrane structure, which cause membrane injury, electrolyte leakage (EL) and cell death. PMC9183074.p025.s004: MDA content and EL are sufficient indicators of membrane thermostability, and they can be considered directly related to the degree of plant damage by HT. PMC9183074.p025.s005: In this study, CN1 and N22 exhibited slightly decreased membrane thermostability, as indicated by a small increase in EL and MDA content (Figure 2), but IR64 and aromatic Thai rice KDML105 displayed a sharp increase in EL and MDA content under HT (Figure 2). PMC9183074.p025.s006: Pansarakham et al. previously reported that MDA and EL of rice cvs. PMC9183074.p025.s007: IR64, KDML105, and PTT1 significantly increased after an exposure to 42 °C for 7 days while those of Dular and N22 remained unchanged.

events.event_label: ROS interacts_with unsaturated fatty acids
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p005.s001: The cellular membrane is the most sensitive site affected by environmental stimulation, including HT. PMC9183074.p005.s002: HT has been reported to be associated with disrupting cell membranes, breaking down cell compartmentalization, leading to solute leakage and affecting cellular functions that cause physiological injuries and leaf senescence. PMC9183074.p005.s003: The disruption of a cellular membrane by HT is related to the increase in membrane lipid peroxidation, which occurs when double bonds of unsaturated fatty acids are attacked by ROS.

events.event_label: HT reduces_or_impairs spikelet fertility
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p007.s003: Rice can be cultivated and grows well in a wide geographical and climatic range. PMC9183074.p007.s004: Elevated temperature can impose adverse effects on rice at different growth stages (such as germination, seedling, tillering, reproductive and grain filling stages) and induce reductions in rice yield. PMC9183074.p007.s005: At the flowering stage, HT reduces spikelet fertility by inhibiting anther dehiscence and hence decreasing pollination. PMC9183074.p007.s006: Additionally, at panicle initiation (PI), HT causes spikelet degeneration and damages the development of flower organs. PMC9183074.p007.s007: The reduction in spikelet fertility can lead to a severe reduction in rice yield.

events.event_label: HT reduces_or_impairs PSII efficiency
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p007.s006: Additionally, at panicle initiation (PI), HT causes spikelet degeneration and damages the development of flower organs. PMC9183074.p007.s007: The reduction in spikelet fertility can lead to a severe reduction in rice yield. PMC9183074.p007.s008: Several reports in rice and other plants showed that HT adversely affected PSII efficiency. PMC9183074.p007.s009: A lower maximum quantum yield of PSII efficiency (Fv/Fm) was found in tall fescue grass exposed to 35/30 °C (day/night) for 20 days, and in spinach stressed at 50 °C. PMC9183074.p007.s010: In rice, significantly reduced Fv/Fm was reported when plants were subjected to heat stress at 40–55 °C for 30 min at seedling, 35 and 40 °C at tillering, 45 °C for 30 min at heading and 42 °C for 30 min at dough grain stage.

events.event_label: MDA positively_correlates_with peroxidation of membrane lipids
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p012.s003: The membranes of the heat-sensitive check, IR64, was most seriously damaged, showing a nine-fold increase in EL, while those of N22 showed the highest integrity with only a 7.15% increase in EL. PMC9183074.p012.s004: Of the two Thai cultivars, CN1 maintained greater membrane stability with its EL value similar to that of N22. PMC9183074.p012.s005: Loss of membrane stability is partly imposed by peroxidation of membrane lipids, which can be indicated by malondialdehyde content. PMC9183074.p012.s006: In the non-stress plants, leaf MDA contents in the four rice genotypes were in the range of 19.19 to 20.63 g gFW−1 (Figure 2B), with KDML105 having slightly higher values than others. PMC9183074.p012.s007: HT stress resulted in significantly increased MDA contents in CN1, KDML105 and IR64.

events.event_label: leaf MDA contents positively_correlates_with peroxidation of membrane lipids
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p012.s003: The membranes of the heat-sensitive check, IR64, was most seriously damaged, showing a nine-fold increase in EL, while those of N22 showed the highest integrity with only a 7.15% increase in EL. PMC9183074.p012.s004: Of the two Thai cultivars, CN1 maintained greater membrane stability with its EL value similar to that of N22. PMC9183074.p012.s005: Loss of membrane stability is partly imposed by peroxidation of membrane lipids, which can be indicated by malondialdehyde content. PMC9183074.p012.s006: In the non-stress plants, leaf MDA contents in the four rice genotypes were in the range of 19.19 to 20.63 g gFW−1 (Figure 2B), with KDML105 having slightly higher values than others. PMC9183074.p012.s007: HT stress resulted in significantly increased MDA contents in CN1, KDML105 and IR64.

events.event_label: HT decreases_abundance_of Total fatty acids
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p014.s001: Total fatty acids in seedlings of all four rice cultivars after an exposure to HT sharply reduced by 1.4–2.0-fold compared to the controls (Figure 3). PMC9183074.p014.s002: Significant differences in total fatty acids were noted among genotypes. PMC9183074.p014.s003: The highest total fatty acid content was found in CN1 for both the non-stress (10.769 µg µL−1) and HT-stressed (7.608 µg µL−1) plants. PMC9183074.p014.s004: The HT-stressed plants of N22, KDML105 and IR 64 had similar contents of total fatty acids, varying from 3.89 to 7.61 µg µL−1.

PMC9183074.p020.s004: Polyunsaturated fatty acids in seedlings of all rice cultivars markedly decreased under HT. PMC9183074.p020.s005: Thai rice, CN1 exhibited the highest level of polyunsaturated fatty acid compared to other cultivars in both control and HT.

events.event_label: HT affects rice
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p022.s001: The highest Sat:Unsat under both control and HT condition were found in KDML105 (Table 3). PMC9183074.p022.s002: Sharp increases in Sat:Unsat after exposure to HT occurred in all rice cultivars in the range between 1.5–1.8-fold compared to the controls. PMC9183074.p022.s003: The highest increase in Sat:Unsat was found in N22, and the lowest increase was found in CN1.

events.event_label: heat stress affects thylakoid membranes
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p027.s001: Most previous studies focused on the changes in lipid composition of isolated thylakoid membranes under heat stress. PMC9183074.p027.s002: Our study analysed fatty acid compositions of the whole leaf; therefore, many fatty acids not previously reported in heat-stress studies were identified and displayed some interesting patterns of responses. PMC9183074.p027.s003: Among saturated fatty acids, arachidic acid (C20:0), behenic acid (C22:0) and lignoceric acid (24:0) (Table 1) sharply increased in N22, CN1 and IR64, while these acids significantly reduced in KDML105. PMC9183074.p027.s004: In addition, erucic acid (C22:3), a long-chain unsaturated fatty acid, increased in response to HT in N22 and CN1, but significantly reduced in KDML105 and IR64 (Table 2).

events.event_label: fatty acid compositions is_detected_in leaf
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p027.s001: Most previous studies focused on the changes in lipid composition of isolated thylakoid membranes under heat stress. PMC9183074.p027.s002: Our study analysed fatty acid compositions of the whole leaf; therefore, many fatty acids not previously reported in heat-stress studies were identified and displayed some interesting patterns of responses. PMC9183074.p027.s003: Among saturated fatty acids, arachidic acid (C20:0), behenic acid (C22:0) and lignoceric acid (24:0) (Table 1) sharply increased in N22, CN1 and IR64, while these acids significantly reduced in KDML105. PMC9183074.p027.s004: In addition, erucic acid (C22:3), a long-chain unsaturated fatty acid, increased in response to HT in N22 and CN1, but significantly reduced in KDML105 and IR64 (Table 2).

events.event_label: HT increases_abundance_of capric acid
events.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p027.s009: Moreover, saturated fatty acid, namely capric acid (C10:0), under HT and control was found in Thai rice, CN1 and KDML105. PMC9183074.p027.s010: In particular, KDML105 showed the highest capric acid content, and it increased significantly after exposure to HT (Figure 5, Table 2). PMC9183074.p027.s011: Capric acid (C10:0) was not found in N22 and IR64. PMC9183074.p027.s012: Capric acid (C10:0) occurs in coconut oil, and it is used as an aromatic chemical in the manufacture of perfumes. PMC9183074.p027.s013: This suggested that Thai rice, CN1 and KDML105, especially KDML105 or Thai Jasmine rice, is an aromatic rice and has volatile aromatic compounds that make the aroma and flavour in the rice.

relations.subject: HT

evidence.text: Under HT, N22 showed the highest heat tolerance displaying the lowest increase in electrolyte leakage (EL), no increments in malondialdehyde (MDA) and stable maximum quantum yield of PSII efficiency (Fv/Fm).
Compared to KDML105 and IR64, CN1 was more tolerant of HT, showing a lower increase in EL and MDA, and less reduction in Fv/Fm.

relations.evidence_preview: Under HT, N22 showed the highest heat tolerance displaying the lowest increase in electrolyte leakage (EL), no increments in malondialdehyde (MDA) and stable maximum quantum yield of PSII efficiency (Fv/Fm). Compared to KDML105 and IR64, CN1 was more tolerant of HT, showing a lower increase in EL and MDA, and less reduction in Fv/Fm.
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p002.s003: Twenty-eight-day-old seedlings of two Thai rice cultivars (CN1 and KDML105), a standard heat tolerance (N22), and a heat sensitive (IR64) rice genotype were treated at 42 °C for 7 days. PMC9183074.p002.s004: Under HT, N22 showed the highest heat tolerance displaying the lowest increase in electrolyte leakage (EL), no increments in malondialdehyde (MDA) and stable maximum quantum yield of PSII efficiency (Fv/Fm). PMC9183074.p002.s005: Compared to KDML105 and IR64, CN1 was more tolerant of HT, showing a lower increase in EL and MDA, and less reduction in Fv/Fm. PMC9183074.p002.s006: N22 and CN1 showed a higher percentage reduction of unsaturated fatty acids (C18:2 and C18:3), which are the major components of the thylakoid membrane, rendering the optimum thylakoid membrane fluidity and intactness of PSII complex. PMC9183074.p002.s007: Moreover, they exhibited sharp increases in long-chain fatty acids, particularly C22:1, while the heat sensitive IR64 and KDML105 showed significant reductions.

evidence_context_sentences.text: Under HT, N22 showed the highest heat tolerance displaying the lowest increase in electrolyte leakage (EL), no increments in malondialdehyde (MDA) and stable maximum quantum yield of PSII efficiency (Fv/Fm).
Compared to KDML105 and IR64, CN1 was more tolerant of HT, showing a lower increase in EL and MDA, and less reduction in Fv/Fm.

N22 and CN1 showed a higher percentage reduction of unsaturated fatty acids (C18:2 and C18:3), which are the major components of the thylakoid membrane, rendering the optimum thylakoid membrane fluidity and intactness of PSII complex.
Moreover, they exhibited sharp increases in long-chain fatty acids, particularly C22:1, while the heat sensitive IR64 and KDML105 showed significant reductions.
Dramatic increases in long-chain fatty acids may lead to cuticular wax synthesis which provides protective roles for heat tolerance.

A temperature greater than 40 °C affects many crops by causing thermal injury, inhibiting photosynthesis and changing protein structure and function such as protein unfolding, denaturation, aggregation and inactivation of enzyme reactions.
The first and highly sensitive photosynthetic site which is affected by HT is photosystem II (PSII).
HT affects PSII by significantly decreasing its function.

Several aspects of HT effects on PSII have been reported, including the disassociation of the PSII peripheral antenna complex from the core complex, inactivation and disassociation of oxygen-evolving PSII complexes, and inhibition of electron transport from QA to QB.

HT led to changes in lipid membrane structure and membrane fluidity relating to changes in lipid composition and saturation level.
Increased lipid saturation level was reported in creeping bentgrass exposed to high soil temperature (35 °C) or high ambient temperature (35 °C).
In tall fescue turfgrass, increased lipid unsaturation was reported when exposed to HT (40/35 °C, 14/10 h), but in wheat under HT, there was a decline in the degree of lipid unsaturation.
Several reports pronounced the alteration of lipid composition upon heat stress such as the increase in degree of saturated fatty acids or decrease in unsaturated fatty acids.

relations.triple: control condition associates_with maximum quantum yield of PSII efficiency
relations.subject: control condition
relations.object: maximum quantum yield of PSII efficiency

evidence.text: Rice plants of all four cultivars in the control condition had the maximum quantum yield of PSII efficiency in the dark-adapted state (Fv/Fm) values equal to or higher than 0.80, indicating the absence of damage to PSII, with CN1 showing the highest value at 0.822 (Figure 1).

relations.evidence_preview: Rice plants of all four cultivars in the control condition had the maximum quantum yield of PSII efficiency in the dark-adapted state (Fv/Fm) values equal to or higher than 0.80, indicating the absence of damage to PSII, with CN1 showing the highest value at 0.822 (Figure 1).
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p010.s001: Rice plants of all four cultivars in the control condition had the maximum quantum yield of PSII efficiency in the dark-adapted state (Fv/Fm) values equal to or higher than 0.80, indicating the absence of damage to PSII, with CN1 showing the highest value at 0.822 (Figure 1). PMC9183074.p010.s002: However, when subjected to HT stress, Fv/Fm significantly decreased in IR64 (the heat sensitive check), KDML105 and CN1. PMC9183074.p010.s003: In contrast, the heat-tolerant genotype (N22) maintained a high Fv/Fm value (approximately 0.806). relations.subject: control condition

evidence.text: All four rice genotypes under the control condition displayed good membrane stability as indicated by low EL values ranging from 3.24% to 4.16% (Figure 2A).

relations.evidence_preview: All four rice genotypes under the control condition displayed good membrane stability as indicated by low EL values ranging from 3.24% to 4.16% (Figure 2A).
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p012.s001: All four rice genotypes under the control condition displayed good membrane stability as indicated by low EL values ranging from 3.24% to 4.16% (Figure 2A). PMC9183074.p012.s002: When subjected to HT stress, cellular membranes were damaged, and all rice genotypes showed significantly higher EL. PMC9183074.p012.s003: The membranes of the heat-sensitive check, IR64, was most seriously damaged, showing a nine-fold increase in EL, while those of N22 showed the highest integrity with only a 7.15% increase in EL. PMC9183074.p012.s004: Of the two Thai cultivars, CN1 maintained greater membrane stability with its EL value similar to that of N22.

Palmitic acid (C16:0) was found in the greatest amount under both control and HT conditions.
Palmitic acid and stearic acid markedly decreased with HT in all cultivars.

Three saturated fatty acids including arachidic acid, behenic acid, and lignoceric acid in N22, CN1 and IR64 were significantly increased after HT treatment, but in KDML105, the contents of these acids declined significantly.

relations.subject: HT

evidence.text: It decreases photosynthetic efficiency and yield.

relations.evidence_preview: It decreases photosynthetic efficiency and yield.
relations.evidence_context_text: PMC9183074.p024.s001: High temperature (HT) is harmful to plant growth and survival. PMC9183074.p024.s002: It decreases photosynthetic efficiency and yield. PMC9183074.p024.s003: HT can induce excessive reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation, resulting in cellular membrane injury, impaired photosynthetic mechanism and oxygen evolving complex associated with PSII, and inhibition of protein synthesis. PMC9183074.p024.s004: Plants can adapt to HT by changing the morphology and altering metabolism via signal transduction.

evidence_context_sentences.text: High temperature (HT) is harmful to plant growth and survival. It decreases photosynthetic efficiency and yield.

HT can induce excessive reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation, resulting in cellular membrane injury, impaired photosynthetic mechanism and oxygen evolving complex associated with PSII, and inhibition of protein synthesis.
Plants can adapt to HT by changing the morphology and altering metabolism via signal transduction.
The chloroplast photosynthetic function is supposed to be the most HT-sensitive cellular function, and PSII is one of the most sensitive to HT.

PMC9183074.p024.s006: In this study, PSII efficiency was determined by measuring the maximum quantum yield in the dark-adapted stage (Fv/Fm), which decreased significantly after exposure to HT in CN1, KDML105 and IR64 but remained unchanged in the heat-tolerant genotype, N22 (Figure 1). evidence_context_sentences.text: Plants can adapt to HT by changing the morphology and altering metabolism via signal transduction.
The chloroplast photosynthetic function is supposed to be the most HT-sensitive cellular function, and PSII is one of the most sensitive to HT.

In this study, PSII efficiency was determined by measuring the maximum quantum yield in the dark-adapted stage (Fv/Fm), which decreased significantly after exposure to HT in CN1, KDML105 and IR64 but remained unchanged in the heat-tolerant genotype, N22 (Figure 1).

Heat-induced reduction in Fv/Fm resulted from an increase in minimum fluorescence in the dark-adapted state (F0) which indicated the inhibition of electron transport and a reduction in maximum fluorescence in the dark-adapted state (Fm) that is caused by a dissociation of peripheral antenna complex of PSII from its core complex.
Similarly, Dongsansuk et al. reported that Fv/Fm significantly reduced in the heat-sensitive rice PT60 but was stable in Dular after exposure to 45 °C for 30 min.
